title: Film Density Measurement
narrationScript: Everything in film radiography funnels down to one number. Density. It decides whether the radiograph is even allowed to be interpreted, and it tells you whether your exposure and your processing did what you planned. So a Level two has to own two skills. Reading density precisely with a densitometer, and estimating it sensibly with a step wedge comparison film. Start with what density actually is. Photographic density is the logarithm, base ten, of the ratio between the light hitting the back of the film and the light that makes it through. The log scale is the part that trips people up. A density of one means ten percent of the light gets through. Density two means one percent. Density three means one tenth of one percent. Density four means one part in ten thousand. Every whole step is another factor of ten. That is why a film at density four looks nearly black on an ordinary view box, and why high intensity illuminators exist. Codes put working windows on density because film only performs in the middle of its range. Typical single film limits run from about one point eight for X ray techniques, or two point zero for gamma, up to four point zero for either, measured through the body of the image quality indicator and through the area of interest. Below the minimum you are reading the toe of the characteristic curve, where contrast collapses. Above the maximum, most viewers cannot push enough light through the film to read it. Codes also commonly limit how much the density may vary across the area of interest compared with the density at the image quality indicator, on the order of minus fifteen percent to plus thirty percent. The precision tool is the transmission densitometer. Inside, it is simple. A stabilized lamp shines up through a small aperture, typically a couple of millimeters across. The film lies over the aperture. A photodetector in the arm above measures the light that survives the trip, and a log circuit converts the ratio straight into density on the display. Using one well is a discipline. Let the unit warm up. Zero it with nothing over the aperture. Then verify it against a calibrated step tablet, which is a strip of film steps whose densities are certified traceable to a national standard. Typical code practice calls for that verification at the start of each shift, again after about eight hours of continuous use, and any time you change the aperture, with readings agreeing within plus or minus five hundredths of the certified values. Many written practices also calibrate the instrument on a set interval, on the order of every ninety days while in use. Keep the aperture and the film clean, press the arm down firmly and squarely, and take the readings your procedure requires. Through the body of the image quality indicator. Through the area of interest. And at the lightest and densest spots you intend to accept. Treat the step tablet like the instrument it is. Handle it by the edges, keep it in its sleeve, and retire it when it gets scratched or worn, because a damaged standard quietly miscalibrates every reading that follows it. The estimating tool is the step wedge comparison film. It is a strip of uniform density patches, manufactured or certified so that each patch value is known. You lay it on the illuminator beside the production film, slide it until a patch matches the area you care about, and read off that patch value. Done carefully, with a clean strip, an even illuminator, and adapted eyes, a practiced reader gets within a tenth or two of the true density. Many codes accept density determination by comparison film, provided the strip itself is verified, typically once a year, against a calibrated densitometer. But understand its limits. Matching is subjective. It depends on your eyes, your adaptation, and the evenness of the illuminator. Scratched or faded strips lie to you. When acceptance hangs on a tenth of density, or when a customer review is coming, the calibrated densitometer is the referee. One last habit separates professionals from film handlers. Record what you measure. A density number that lives only in your head is not a quality record. Write the readings on the reader sheet against each film, note which instrument you used and when it was last verified, and the radiograph becomes what the code intends it to be. An objective, defensible record. Density is the cheapest measurement in radiography, and measured right, it protects every other judgment you will make on that film.
imagePrompt: A technician presses the reading arm of a tabletop transmission densitometer onto a weld radiograph at a darkroom bench, with a sleeved calibrated step tablet and a glowing film illuminator beside the instrument.

sections.heading: Density: The Gatekeeper Number
sections.body:
Photographic **density** is the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of light incident on the film to light transmitted through it: D = log₁₀(I₀/I_t). The log scale is what trips people up:

- D = 1.0 → 10 % of the light gets through
- D = 2.0 → 1 %
- D = 3.0 → 0.1 %
- D = 4.0 → 0.01 %

Each whole step is another **factor of ten**. A film at D = 4.0 passes one ten-thousandth of the viewing light - which is why high-intensity illuminators exist and why an ordinary view box goes useless somewhere above D ≈ 2.5.

Density is the gatekeeper measurement of film radiography: it confirms that exposure and processing landed where planned, and no interpretation is valid until the density of the area of interest is confirmed inside the code window. Two tools do the job - the **densitometer** for quantitative readings and the **step-wedge comparison film** for visual estimates - and a Level II is expected to use, verify, and know the limits of both.

sections.caption: Density is a log scale: each unit of density cuts transmitted light by a factor of ten, and codes confine acceptable films to a window measured through the area of interest.

sections.heading: Code Density Windows
sections.body:
Codes such as ASME Section V set density limits measured **through the body of the IQI and through the area of interest**. Typical single-film viewing windows: minimum **1.8** for X-ray techniques and **2.0** for gamma, maximum **4.0** for either. For **composite viewing** of double-film exposures, the superimposed set must commonly reach at least **2.6**, with each film of the set at least **1.3** so either film can also be handled alone.

The physics behind the window: below the minimum you are reading the **toe** of the characteristic curve, where the gradient - and with it your flaw sensitivity - collapses; above the maximum, most illuminators cannot drive enough light through the film.

Codes also bound **density variation**: a common rule allows the density through the area of interest to vary from **−15 % to +30 %** relative to the density through the IQI body. Aim for the meat of the window - roughly 2.0 to 3.5 - rather than skating the limits: that buys margin for densitometer tolerance, shading across the part, and the next reviewer's instrument.

sections.heading: The Transmission Densitometer
sections.body:
A transmission densitometer is four parts in a hinged frame:

- a **stabilized light source** beneath the stage,
- a small **aperture** (commonly 1-3 mm) that defines the measured spot,
- a **photodetector** in the reading arm above,
- a **log-converting circuit** and display that turn the measured light ratio directly into density.

Good technique is a fixed ritual. Power on and let the unit **warm up**. **Zero (null)** it with nothing over the aperture. **Verify** it against a calibrated step tablet. Keep film and stage clean - dirt over a 2 mm aperture becomes part of the reading. Center the point of interest over the aperture and press the arm down **firmly and squarely**; a tilted or half-closed arm leaks ambient light to the detector and reads low.

Take the readings the procedure requires: through the IQI body, through the area of interest, and at the lightest and densest areas you intend to accept. Record every reading on the reader sheet with the instrument identification - a density that was never written down never happened.

sections.caption: Inside a transmission densitometer - stabilized lamp, defining aperture, photodetector, and log readout - verified against a traceable calibrated step tablet.

sections.heading: Verification Against a Calibrated Step Tablet
sections.body:
A densitometer is only as honest as its last verification. The reference is a **calibrated step tablet** (step-wedge calibration film): a strip of density steps certified traceable to a national standard. Typical code practice, in the style of ASME Section V:

- **Calibrate** the densitometer against the tablet at set intervals - on the order of every 90 days while in use.
- **Verify** at the **start of each shift**, after about **8 hours of continuous use**, and after any **aperture change**.
- Acceptance: readings within **±0.05** of the certified step values.

Treat the tablet like the instrument it is: handle it by the edges, keep it sleeved, never write on it, and retire it when scratched, stained, or worn - a damaged standard silently miscalibrates every film judged after it. Log each verification with date, instrument, tablet serial, and readings. If verification fails, the unit is adjusted or repaired and re-verified before any production film is measured - and readings taken since the last good check become suspect.

sections.title: Typical verification cadence
sections.body: Codes commonly require the densitometer to be verified against a national-standard-traceable step tablet at the **start of each shift**, after **8 hours of continuous use**, or after an **aperture change** - with agreement within **±0.05** - and step-wedge **comparison** films to be verified at least **annually** against a calibrated densitometer.

sections.heading: Step-Wedge Comparison Films
sections.body:
The step-wedge comparison film is the estimating tool: a strip of uniform patches of known density, laid on the illuminator beside the production film and slid along until a patch visually matches the area of interest. The matched patch's value is the estimate. Done carefully - clean strip, even illuminator, eyes adapted - a practiced reader lands within about 0.1 to 0.2 of true density.

Many codes accept density determination by comparison film, provided the strip is **verified annually** against a calibrated densitometer (and re-verified if damaged). Its limits are built in:

- matching is **subjective** and depends on the reader's eyes and adaptation,
- an uneven illuminator skews the match,
- scratched, faded, or stained strips lie,
- interpolating between patches is a guess.

Use it for quick checks, field screening, and confirming that a densitometer has not drifted absurdly. When acceptance hangs on a tenth of density - or a customer review looms - the calibrated densitometer is the referee.

sections.caption: Densitometer versus step-wedge comparison film.
rows: Photoelectric measurement of transmitted light with log readout
Visual side-by-side match against patches of known density
Each shift / 8 h / aperture change against a traceable step tablet; periodic calibration
Verified annually against a calibrated densitometer
Fast, cheap, no power - good for field screening
Dirty aperture, poor arm contact, skipped warm-up
Subjective match, uneven illuminator, worn or faded strips

figures.title: Film density is a log scale

interactive.title: Density Check: A Shift on the Densitometer
interactive.intro: You are the Level II reading films at the start of the night shift. The densitometer sits on the bench beside a sleeved calibrated step tablet and a step-wedge comparison strip.

steps.prompt: Before the first production film, what comes first?
options: Measure the most urgent film while the unit warms up
Warm up, zero with nothing over the aperture, then verify against the calibrated step tablet
Start reading - verification was done last week
Compare the step tablet to the comparison strip
steps.feedback: Typical code practice requires verification at the start of each shift, after about 8 hours of use, or after an aperture change. The ritual is warm-up, zero, then read the traceable tablet.

steps.prompt: The 2.00 step reads 2.08. Your procedure allows ±0.05. What now?
options: Proceed - 0.08 is close enough for weld film
Mentally subtract 0.08 from every reading tonight
Stop, adjust or recalibrate per the manual, and re-verify before reading any production film
Verify against a different step that agrees better
steps.feedback: Out of tolerance means out of service. Adjust or repair, re-verify against the tablet, and only then measure production film. Mental corrections and cherry-picked steps are not calibration.

steps.prompt: Verification now passes. On a gamma weld radiograph, where do you take the density readings?
options: One reading anywhere convenient on the film
Through the IQI body and through the area of interest, including the lightest and densest areas you intend to accept
Only over the location markers
On the comparison strip lying next to the film
steps.feedback: The window - typically 2.0 to 4.0 for gamma - is judged through the IQI body and the area of interest, and the variation limits are judged against the IQI-body density.

steps.prompt: A film reads 4.3 in the area of interest. The shooter says the big illuminator is bright enough to read it. Your call?
options: Accept - viewer brightness solves everything
Accept if the IQI is visible somewhere on the film
Reject for that area: density exceeds the typical 4.0 single-film code maximum, so the area must be re-shot with a corrected technique
Set it aside and read it tomorrow with fresh eyes
steps.feedback: Above the code maximum, the film is not acceptable for that area no matter how strong the light feels. The exposure technique must put density back inside the window.

steps.prompt: Mid-shift the densitometer fails. A certified step-wedge comparison strip is on the bench. What can you legitimately do?
options: Use the strip for density determination only if the governing code and your procedure permit it, and the strip carries a current verification
Use the strip for everything - comparison strips never need verification
Estimate density by eye; you read films all day
Release the films unread and note the instrument failure later
steps.feedback: Many codes do allow density determination by comparison film, but your procedure decides, and the strip must hold a current (typically annual) verification against a calibrated densitometer.

checkQuestions.question: A film area transmits 1 % of the illuminator's light. Its density is:
checkQuestions.explanation: D = log₁₀(I₀/I_t) = log₁₀(100/1) = 2.0 - each whole density step is a factor of ten in transmitted light.

checkQuestions.question: A spot on a film passes 0.5 % of the viewing light. The density there is closest to:
checkQuestions.explanation: D = log₁₀(100/0.5) = log₁₀(200) ≈ 2.3 - halving the transmitted light adds about 0.3 to the density.

checkQuestions.question: Per typical code practice, a densitometer must be verified against a traceable calibrated step tablet:
options: Once a year only
At the start of each shift, after about 8 hours of continuous use, or after an aperture change - within ±0.05
Only when readings start to look wrong
Every five years at the factory
checkQuestions.explanation: Shift-start, 8-hour, and aperture-change verification within ±0.05 of the certified steps is the common code pattern, with periodic calibration on the order of every 90 days in use.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about step-wedge comparison films is correct?
options: They are more precise than a calibrated densitometer
They may be used for density determination where codes permit, provided they are verified - typically annually - against a calibrated densitometer
They need no verification because the densities are printed on them
They are required for composite viewing of double-film exposures
checkQuestions.explanation: Comparison films are a legitimate, code-recognized tool, but they are subjective estimates and must carry a current verification against a calibrated densitometer.

checkQuestions.question: A production weld film shot with a gamma source must typically fall in which single-film density window, measured through the IQI body and the area of interest?
checkQuestions.explanation: Typical code limits are 2.0 minimum for gamma techniques (1.8 for X-ray) and 4.0 maximum for single-film viewing - below that contrast collapses on the toe, above it illuminators run out of light.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §2.8 (film density, step-wedge comparison films, densitometers)
ASME Section V-style density limits, density variation, and densitometer/step-wedge verification practice (generic reference)
Photographic density definition D = log₁₀(I₀/I_t) (reference value)
